paragraphs.body: Barriers to Employment: The Shifting Value of International Students  Barriers to Employment: The Shifting Value of International Students Introduction How does it feel to graduate from a foreign university, only to face unexpected obstacles in landing a job? For many Chinese international students, this is a reality that comes with navigating cultural differences, visa restrictions, and employer biases. This article will discuss the challenges faced by Chinese international students in finding employment and whether their value in the job market is underestimated. I believe that international students have unique value in any job market and their value should not be underestimated. First, the article will discuss the main obstacles faced by Chinese international students when working in the countries where they studied, including visa restrictions, cultural differences, and local employer preferences. This will assess whether their employment value is lower than that of local graduates. Secondly, the article will discuss the employment situation of Chinese international students after returning to China to analyze the phenomenon that the competitiveness of overseas academic qualifications in the domestic market has declined in recent years. Through this analysis, the impact of this change on the employment of international students will be explored. In addition, the article will also propose the unique advantages of Chinese international students in the job market, including cross-cultural adaptability, bilingual skills, and international perspectives. Finally, the article will explore some ways to help international students cope with employment challenges, such as accumulating local experience through internships or part-time jobs and highlighting unique advantages in job hunting to enhance competitiveness. Main Body First, visa restrictions are a major obstacle for Chinese students to find employment in countries where they study (such as the United States, the United Kingdom, and Australia). Pang, Perrone, and Wong (2021) pointed out that many Australian employers are uneasy about the complex visa application process and are reluctant to hire international students. In addition, cultural differences and language barriers further limit the workplace performance of international students. Yu and Moskal (2018) found that Chinese students lack cross-cultural interaction with local students in British universities, and this lack carries over to the workplace, making it difficult for them to integrate into the team. On the other hand, employer preferences also affect the employment opportunities of Chinese students. Studies have shown that many employers in the United States have more confidence in local graduates and believe that they understand the local workplace culture better (Heng, 2019). Therefore, Chinese students are often considered to lack skills that match the local market. These challenges combined have led to the underestimation of the employment value of international students in the countries where they study.

paragraphs.body: At the same time, Chinese students also face the problem of increased competition after returning home. As more and more graduates choose to return home, the advantages of overseas academic qualifications may be weakening. Zhao and Kung (2021) pointed out that domestic employers now pay more attention to actual work experience rather than academic background. That is, many employers believe that although international students have overseas education experience, they lack practical experience related to the domestic market. In addition, international students may take part-time jobs due to financial difficulties during their studies. If the students rely too much on part-time work, their academic performance may be affected (Thies, 2023). This problem will also affect their employment competitiveness after returning home, because employers may pay more attention to complete academic records. These problems have limited their development in their career path to a certain extent.

Nevertheless, I believe that Chinese international students still have unique value that cannot be ignored. Their cross-cultural adaptability makes them a bridge connecting different markets. Although Chinese international students face a lack of cross-cultural interaction in the UK, their cross-cultural communication skills demonstrated in international companies have been recognized (Yu & Moskal, 2018). Pang et al.'s research in 2021 further shows that in the Australian workplace, the bilingual ability of international students is crucial to the international development of enterprises. Heng also emphasized in the article that the international vision of Chinese international students enables them to demonstrate strong innovation capabilities when solving cross-cultural problems. These advantages are particularly important in the context of globalization, especially for students who want to enter international companies.

annotations.comment: Please indicate the year of publication.

paragraphs.body: In response to the employment challenges faced by Chinese international students, some strategies that can help them are worth promoting. First, I believe that international universities should strengthen career services, such as providing visa guidance, mock interviews, and cross-cultural training. Yu and Moskal suggested that schools can promote the interaction between international students and local students through more cross-cultural activities to enhance their cultural adaptability. Second, appropriate participation in internships and part-time jobs is an effective way to accumulate local experience. Thies's research shows that targeted part-time experience can help students build confidence in the workplace. In this way, international students can more actively demonstrate their unique value in the job search process. For example, cross-cultural adaptability, bilingual skills, and international vision. Finally, for returned international students, adapting to the domestic workplace culture is particularly important. Zhao and Kung suggest that returned students can understand the needs of the domestic market through industry seminars or alumni networks and adjust their job search strategies accordingly.

paragraphs.body: There are also some counterarguments on this topic, such as Local Preference. My response is that although employers prefer local graduates, international students' cross-cultural skills and global perspectives are not easy for local students to have. Although visa issues increase costs, many international students have legal work permits, and their contributions often exceed these costs. In the global market, bilingualism and international perspectives are very important, especially in multinational companies. Cultural barriers do exist, but the experience of studying abroad makes international students more adaptable and can bring different perspectives to the team. All of these show the unique value of international students. Conclusion Chinese international students face multiple challenges in the global job market, but they still show irreplaceable value. Through effective educational support, practical opportunities and self-improvement, international students can better cope with these difficulties and stand out in the workplace. In the context of globalization, international students, as the main role of cross-cultural communication, will continue to have a positive impact on society.

annotations.text: My response is that although employers prefer local graduates, international students' cross-cultural skills and global perspectives are not easy for local students to have.
